# NO AI ACT

> NO AI ACT is a free, open-source investigative serious game about the European
> AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689). The player acts as an inspector: they open
> the file of an artificial intelligence system, examine the evidence, cite the
> proof and file an inspection report classifying the system's risk (prohibited,
> high-risk, to be governed, or compliant). It is an educational simulation about
> algorithmic risk governance — not legal advice.

- Version: v0.6.0
- Status: public release
- Languages: Italian (default) and English
- Audience: upper-secondary schools, universities, professional training, and professionals working with AI governance
- Pricing: free and open source (MIT code, CC BY 4.0 educational content)
- 11 playable cases

## Key facts

- Educational serious game about the European AI Act and algorithmic risk governance.
- It is NOT legal advice and does not replace the regulation or a professional opinion.
- No backend, no account, no personal data collection in the game.
- Public pages use aggregate, privacy-friendly Cloudflare Web Analytics (no cookies, no personal data); the game itself sends no analytics, results, answers or reports. Progress is stored only in the browser's localStorage.
- Teacher mode is local; its debrief export is local and anonymous.
- The Tally playtest form is an external, voluntary feedback form, separate from the
  game; it does not receive any personal data automatically and is not required to play.

## Links

- Italian landing page: https://matteoangeloni-jpeg.github.io/-NO-AI-ACT/
- English landing page: https://matteoangeloni-jpeg.github.io/-NO-AI-ACT/en/
- Play the game: https://matteoangeloni-jpeg.github.io/-NO-AI-ACT/play/
- GitHub repository: https://github.com/matteoangeloni-jpeg/-NO-AI-ACT
- GitHub Release v0.6.0: https://github.com/matteoangeloni-jpeg/-NO-AI-ACT/releases/tag/v0.6.0
- Project brief: https://github.com/matteoangeloni-jpeg/-NO-AI-ACT/blob/main/docs/NO_AI_ACT_PROJECT_BRIEF.md
- Teacher quick start: https://github.com/matteoangeloni-jpeg/-NO-AI-ACT/blob/main/docs/TEACHER_QUICK_START.md
- Legal disclaimer: https://github.com/matteoangeloni-jpeg/-NO-AI-ACT/blob/main/docs/LEGAL_DISCLAIMER.md

## What it teaches

- Risk classification of AI systems under the European AI Act.
- Prohibited AI practices (e.g. social scoring) and why they are banned.
- High-risk systems (e.g. healthcare, education, public administration) and their obligations.
- Transparency duties, effective human oversight and deployer responsibility.
- Why a disclaimer or a merely formal human check is often not enough.
- How the context of use changes the risk of the same system.
- Contestability: arguing a decision with evidence.

## The 11 cases (Italian title — topic)

1. La città dei punteggi — social scoring and the boundary of prohibited practices.
2. Il colloquio che non esiste — automated recruitment, risks in employment.
3. La città sintetica — generated content and transparency obligations.
4. La classe osservata — monitoring in education, rights of minors.
5. Triage invisibile — high-risk clinical decision support in healthcare.
6. Volti nella folla — biometric recognition in public spaces.
7. Il credito civico — mirror case: prohibited social scoring vs welfare and credit scoring.
8. Lo sportello che risponde sempre — public chatbot, reliance, human channel.
9. La gara opaca — public procurement of AI without documentation or governance.
10. La classe profilata — adaptive learning (EdTech) platform profiling students.
11. Il modello tuttofare — generative model (GPAI) used downstream in internal decisions.

## Concepts covered

prohibited practices, high-risk AI, transparency, GPAI, public chatbot,
AI procurement, adaptive EdTech, deployer responsibility, human oversight,
documentation, contestability, accountability.

## Notes

- NO AI ACT is a didactic simplification and does not replace the AI Act text or professional legal advice.
- A third-party legal review is recommended before any formal institutional use.
- Cases such as GPAI, public chatbots, EdTech and procurement are framed by context of use, not as blanket prohibitions.
- The project is not certified, official or a compliance tool.
